name: Thomas Watson
source_summary: Thomas Watson (c. 1620–1686) was an English Puritan minister and theologian. Educated at Emmanuel College, Cambridge, he served as rector of St Stephen Walbrook in the City of London from 1646 until the Great Ejection of 1662, when he was expelled along with nearly two thousand other nonconformist ministers for refusing to conform to the Act of Uniformity. He continued to preach covertly and publicly when permitted. His major works include A Body of Divinity (sermons on the Westminster Shorter Catechism), All Things for Good (on Romans 8:28), and The Doctrine of Repentance. He died suddenly while in prayer at Barnston, Essex in 1686.
adult_story:
Thomas Watson (c.1620–1686) was an English Puritan minister educated at Emmanuel College, Cambridge — then the nursery of Puritan theology in England. He became rector of St Stephen Walbrook in the City of London in 1646, where he ministered with remarkable effectiveness for sixteen years. In 1662, the Act of Uniformity required all clergy to subscribe to the Book of Common Prayer and accept episcopal ordination. Watson refused. Along with nearly two thousand other Puritan ministers — in what became known as the Great Ejection — he was turned out of his church on a single day, 24 August 1662.

He is known chiefly for three books. A Body of Divinity — sermons on the Westminster Shorter Catechism — remains one of the clearest introductions to Reformed theology in the English language, opening with the question "What is the chief end of man?" and demonstrating that doctrine is not abstract but immediately connected to worship. All Things for Good meditates on Romans 8:28 with the authority of a man who had lost his living; The Doctrine of Repentance carefully distinguishes genuine repentance from its counterfeits.

Watson died suddenly while in prayer at Barnston, Essex in 1686. He was Reformed in theology, Puritan in devotion, and pastoral in care for ordinary believers — always intent on helping them understand not just what they believed, but how to tell whether they had the real thing.
family_story:
Thomas Watson was a Puritan minister in England, born around 1620. He went to Cambridge University and became rector of a church in London.

In 1662 the king required all ministers to use the official prayer book. Watson and nearly two thousand other ministers refused — and all walked out of their churches on the same day. This is called the Great Ejection.

Watson kept preaching and writing. He wrote All Things for Good, based on Romans 8:28 — which says God works everything together for those who love Him. He wrote it while going through great difficulty himself, and it shows. He died in 1686 while praying.

name: Corrie ten Boom
source_summary: Corrie ten Boom (1892–1983) was a Dutch evangelical Christian who, with her family, hid Jewish people in their home in Haarlem during the Nazi occupation of the Netherlands. Betrayed in 1944, she and her sister Betsie were sent to Ravensbrück concentration camp, where Betsie died in December 1944. Corrie was released through a clerical error one week later — one week before all women of her age in the camp were executed. She spent the rest of her life speaking and writing about forgiveness, and wrote The Hiding Place (1971).
adult_story:
Corrie ten Boom was born in Haarlem, Netherlands in 1892, the daughter of a watchmaker. The ten Boom family were devout Dutch Reformed Christians and their home above the shop was a centre of Christian community for decades.

When the Nazis occupied Holland, the family began sheltering Jewish people in a secret room built behind a false wall in Corrie's bedroom. In February 1944 they were betrayed and arrested. Corrie and her sister Betsie were sent to Ravensbrück concentration camp in Germany. Betsie died there in December 1944. Corrie was released the same week — through a clerical error — one week before all women of her age in the camp were executed.

After the war, Corrie spent thirty years travelling the world speaking about forgiveness. In 1947, at a church in Munich, she came face to face with one of the SS guards who had mocked them at Ravensbrück. He extended his hand. She prayed, reached out, and later wrote: "The current started in my shoulder, raced down my arm, sprang into our joined hands. And then this healing warmth seemed to flood my whole being... I forgive you, brother! With all my heart." She had, she said, never felt God's love so intensely as in that moment.
family_story:
Corrie ten Boom grew up in the Netherlands in a family that loved God. When the Nazis invaded Holland, the ten Boom family hid Jewish people in a secret room in their house.

In 1944 they were betrayed. Corrie's sister Betsie died in the concentration camp. Corrie was released through a clerical mistake.

After the war, Corrie came face to face with one of the camp guards. She had to choose to forgive him. She prayed — and did. She later wrote that she had never felt God's love so strongly as in that moment.

name: Eric Liddell
source_summary: Eric Liddell (1902–1945) was a Scottish Christian missionary and athlete who won gold in the 400 metres at the 1924 Paris Olympics after refusing to run in his preferred 100 metres event because the heat was scheduled on a Sunday. Born in Tianjin to Scottish missionaries, he returned to China as a missionary in 1925. He was interned by the Japanese at Weifang camp in Shandong in 1943 and died of a brain tumour in the camp in February 1945, six months before liberation.
adult_story:
Eric Liddell was born in Tianjin, China in 1902, the son of Scottish missionaries. He grew up in Scotland, excelled at rugby and athletics, and by 1924 was Britain's finest sprinter, widely expected to win gold in the 100 metres at the Paris Olympics.

His heat was scheduled for a Sunday. Liddell withdrew. He held the conviction that Sunday was set apart for worship. He switched to the 400 metres — not his event — trained intensively, and won gold, setting a world record.

He returned to China in 1925 as a missionary. When the Japanese invaded in 1937 he sent his wife Florence and their daughters to safety in Canada but remained himself. In 1943 he was interned at the Weifang camp in Shandong, where he organised sport for children, taught science, and continued to preach and counsel. He gave up his place on a prisoner exchange to a pregnant woman. In February 1945 he died of a brain tumour in the camp, aged forty-three — six months before liberation. Fellow internee Langdon Gilkey later described him as the most remarkable person he had ever met.

His life asks a persistent question: what does faithfulness actually cost? Liddell answered it at Paris in 1924 — and answered it again, more quietly, in everything that followed.
family_story:
Eric Liddell was born in China in 1902, the son of Scottish missionaries. He grew up to be Britain's finest sprinter.

In 1924 he was expected to win gold in the 100 metres at the Paris Olympics. But his race was on a Sunday — and Eric believed Sunday should be kept for God, not competition. He refused to run. Instead he entered the 400 metres — not his best event — and won gold anyway, setting a world record.

Eric went back to China as a missionary. When the Japanese invaded, he was put in an internment camp. He died there in 1945, serving those around him, six months before liberation.

name: Elisabeth Elliot
source_summary: Elisabeth Elliot (1926–2015) was an American evangelical author and speaker. Following the death of her husband Jim Elliot, killed with four fellow missionaries by the Waodani people of Ecuador in January 1956, she went to live among the Waodani in 1958 with her daughter Valerie and Rachel Saint. The Waodani came to faith in Christ. She wrote Through Gates of Splendour, Shadow of the Almighty, and A Path Through Suffering, and hosted the radio programme Gateway to Joy.
adult_story:
Elisabeth Elliot was born in Brussels in 1926 to American missionary parents. She studied Greek at Wheaton College, where she met Jim Elliot. On 8 January 1956, Jim and four fellow missionaries were killed by the Waodani people of Ecuador — a tribe that had killed every outsider who approached them. Elisabeth was twenty-nine, with a ten-month-old daughter, Valerie.

She did not go home. In 1958, with Rachel Saint — sister of pilot Nate Saint, also killed — Elisabeth and Valerie went to live among the Waodani. For two years she lived in their community, learning their language. The tribe that had killed five missionaries came to faith in Christ, including several of the men who had carried out the killings.

She left Ecuador in 1963 and spent the rest of her long life writing and speaking. Through Gates of Splendour told the story of the five missionaries. Shadow of the Almighty preserved Jim's letters and journals. A Path Through Suffering drew on all she had lived.

She died in 2015. Her most quoted words: "God is God. Because he is God, he is worthy of my trust and obedience. I will find rest nowhere but in his will."
family_story:
Elisabeth Elliot grew up in an American missionary family and went to Ecuador with her husband Jim Elliot. Jim Elliot is already on this site.

When Jim and four friends were killed by the Waodani people in 1956, Elisabeth was left alone with a tiny baby. She could have gone home. Instead, two years later, she went to live with the Waodani — the very people who had killed her husband.

She learned their language. The Waodani came to faith in Jesus, including some of the men who had done the killing.

Elisabeth spent the rest of her life writing and speaking about trusting God even through the worst suffering.

name: Brother Andrew
source_summary: Andrew van der Bijl (1928–2022), known as Brother Andrew, was a Dutch evangelical Christian and founder of Open Doors. Beginning in 1955 he made repeated journeys behind the Iron Curtain into Communist Eastern Europe, carrying Bibles and Christian literature, passing through border controls through what he described as answered prayer. His autobiography God's Smuggler (1967), co-written with John and Elizabeth Sherrill, sold more than ten million copies. He later worked to bring Bibles and the gospel to the Muslim world. He died in Harderwijk, Netherlands in September 2022, aged ninety-four.
adult_story:
Andrew van der Bijl was born in Sint Pancras, Netherlands in 1928 to a poor family. After a restless youth and a stint in the Dutch colonial army in Indonesia, he came to faith through a missionary tract and trained at a missionary college in Glasgow.

In 1955 he attended a communist youth festival in Warsaw and saw the reality of the persecuted church behind the Iron Curtain. He returned home determined to act. Over the following years he made repeated journeys into Poland, East Germany, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Yugoslavia, Bulgaria, and the Soviet Union, carrying Bibles hidden in a battered Volkswagen. At every border crossing he prayed: "Lord, you made blind eyes see. Now make seeing eyes blind — do not let the guards see what you do not want them to see." The Bibles crossed borders unseen.

He founded Open Doors in 1955 to support persecuted Christians worldwide, an organisation that today serves believers in over sixty countries. In later years he turned to the Middle East, meeting Hamas and Hezbollah leaders in order to bring the gospel.

He died in Harderwijk in September 2022, aged ninety-four. He wrote: "I believe in the power of the Bible more than any other weapon on earth." A man who spent his life putting God's Word into the hands of those who were dying to have it.
family_story:
Andrew van der Bijl grew up poor in the Netherlands. For a time he felt lost. Then he read a Christian leaflet and came to faith in Jesus.

He discovered that Christians in Communist Eastern Europe could not get Bibles — the government banned them. Andrew decided to take Bibles to them, hidden in his car. At every border crossing he prayed that God would make the guards unable to see the Bibles — and it worked.

He made dozens of trips and founded Open Doors to help persecuted Christians around the world.

He died in 2022, aged ninety-four, getting God's Word to people who risked everything to have it.

name: William Borden
source_summary: William Whiting Borden (1887–1913) was an American missionary candidate and philanthropist, heir to the Borden dairy fortune. At sixteen, during a round-the-world trip, he committed to missionary service. He gave away much of his inheritance, studied at Yale and Princeton Seminary, and sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic in preparation for working among Chinese Muslims in Kansu province. He contracted cerebrospinal meningitis in Cairo and died on 9 April 1913, aged twenty-five. The phrases 'No reserves. No retreat. No regrets.' were found written in his Bible.
adult_story:
William Borden was born in Chicago in 1887, heir to the Borden dairy fortune. At sixteen, his parents gave him a round-the-world trip. Confronted with poverty across the Middle East and Asia, he wrote home that he intended to give his life to missionary service. In his Bible he wrote two words: No reserves.

At Yale he was a gifted scholar and athlete who turned down lucrative prospects and gave away large portions of his inheritance to missions and Christian work. After Princeton Seminary he prepared to serve among Chinese Muslims in Kansu province. In his Bible: No retreat.

In 1912 he sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic on his way to China. He contracted cerebrospinal meningitis in Cairo and died on 9 April 1913, aged twenty-five, never having reached the mission field he had prepared for.

In his Bible, found after his death: No regrets.

Three phrases written across a decade, each at a different moment of cost — the testimony of a life held loosely from the beginning, released further at each step, and given completely at the last. Borden died in a hospital bed in Cairo. The phrase written at the end is what remains.
family_story:
William Borden was born in America in 1887, into a very wealthy family. When he was sixteen he travelled the world — and seeing so much poverty, he decided to give his life to missions. He wrote in his Bible: "No reserves."

At university he gave away much of his money to Christian work. He planned to be a missionary in China. He wrote: "No retreat."

In 1912 he sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic on his way to China. But he caught a serious illness and died in Cairo, aged just twenty-five, before ever reaching China.

In his Bible, found after his death, he had written at the end: "No regrets."

name: Mary Slessor
source_summary: Mary Mitchell Slessor (1848–1915) was a Scottish missionary to Nigeria. Born in Aberdeen and raised in poverty in Dundee, where she worked in a jute mill from age eleven, she served in Old Calabar from 1876 until her death. She moved progressively deeper inland than any previous missionary, living among the Okoyong people and opposing the practice of killing twins. She rescued and adopted many children. In 1892 she became the first woman appointed to a judicial role in the British Empire, serving as magistrate of the Court of Equity in Okoyong.
adult_story:
Mary Slessor was born in Aberdeen in 1848. Her father was an alcoholic shoemaker; the family moved to Dundee where Mary worked in a jute mill from age eleven, twelve-hour shifts, six days a week. Converted through the United Presbyterian Church and inspired by the death of David Livingstone, she offered herself for missionary service in West Africa.

She arrived in Old Calabar, Nigeria in 1876. She mastered the Efik language and moved progressively deeper inland than any previous missionary, eventually living among the Okoyong people far from any European presence. She wore local dress, went barefoot, and lived as her neighbours lived.

In the Calabar region, twins were believed to be cursed. One or both infants would be killed; the mother driven out. When Slessor heard the screaming she ran — sometimes barefoot through the undergrowth — to rescue the babies. She adopted many and raised them herself.

In 1892 she was appointed magistrate of the Court of Equity in Okoyong — the first woman to hold such a role in the British Empire. She died in Nigeria in 1915, having served for thirty-nine years.

A factory girl from Dundee, without formal education or social influence, who changed the culture of a region by refusing to look away from its cruelties.
family_story:
Mary Slessor grew up very poor in Scotland, working in a factory from the age of eleven. She became a Christian, inspired by David Livingstone, and went to Africa.

She went to Nigeria, learned the local language, and moved deep into the jungle — far from any other Europeans. She wore local clothes and went barefoot to be close to the people she served.

In that part of Nigeria, twins were believed to be cursed and were killed at birth. When Mary heard babies crying, she ran into the jungle to rescue them. She saved many, adopting some herself.

Mary served in Nigeria for thirty-nine years and never came home.

name: Adoniram Judson
source_summary: Adoniram Judson (1788–1850) was an American Baptist missionary, the first American Protestant foreign missionary, sent to Burma (Myanmar). He sailed for Burma with his wife Ann in 1812, converting from Congregationalism to Baptist conviction during the voyage. He was imprisoned for seventeen months during the Anglo-Burmese War of 1824–26. He produced the first Burmese Bible (1835) and a Burmese-English dictionary. He lost two wives and several children to illness. By his death in 1850 at sea, there were approximately 8,000 Burmese Christians and 100 churches.
adult_story:
Adoniram Judson was born in Massachusetts in 1788, the son of a minister. He sailed for Burma with his wife Ann in 1812 as one of America's first overseas missionaries, converting from Congregationalism to Baptist conviction during the voyage. His first Burmese convert was not baptised until 1819 — six years after arrival.

The Anglo-Burmese War of 1824 brought catastrophe. Judson was arrested as a suspected spy and imprisoned for seventeen months in Oung-pen-la — Death Prison — where prisoners were chained and suspended from bamboo poles at night. Ann walked to the prison daily, bribing guards for access and nursing their infant daughter Maria through the gates. Ann died in 1826 shortly after his release; Maria died weeks later.

Judson pressed on. He completed the first Burmese Bible in 1835 and compiled a Burmese-English dictionary of 25,000 entries. He lost two more wives in the field. He died at sea in 1850, having never returned to America. By then there were approximately 8,000 Burmese Christians and 100 churches.

"The future is as bright as the promises of God." A man who outlasted every loss not because he was immune to grief but because he had concluded that the gospel was owed to the Burmese regardless of what it cost him.
family_story:
Adoniram Judson sailed from America to Burma in 1812 — one of the first American overseas missionaries. For six years, no one became a Christian.

Then a war broke out and Judson was thrown into a terrible prison. His wife Ann walked to the prison every day to bring him food. When he was finally released, Ann died. Their baby daughter died soon after.

Judson stayed in Burma. He translated the whole Bible into Burmese so people could read it. By the time he died, there were thousands of Burmese Christians and one hundred churches.

He waited six years for his first convert — and never stopped trusting God through every loss.

name: Horatio Spafford
hymns: It Is Well with My Soul
source_summary: Horatio Gates Spafford (1828–1888) was an American lawyer, businessman, and Presbyterian Christian who was a close friend and financial supporter of D.L. Moody. He suffered major financial loss in the Great Chicago Fire of 1871. In November 1873 his four daughters drowned when the ocean liner Ville du Havre sank after a collision. His wife Anna survived. Sailing to meet her, he wrote the words of It Is Well with My Soul, which Philip Bliss set to music. Spafford subsequently lost a son to scarlet fever in 1880 and moved with his wife to Jerusalem in 1881, where he served the poor until his death in 1888.
adult_story:
Horatio Spafford was born in Troy, New York in 1828. A Chicago lawyer and businessman, he was a close friend and financial backer of the evangelist D.L. Moody and a man of active evangelical faith.

In 1871 the Great Chicago Fire consumed most of his real estate investments. In November 1873 he sent his wife Anna and their four daughters — Annie, Maggie, Bessie, and Tanetta — to France on the ocean liner Ville du Havre. He planned to follow shortly. The ship was struck by an iron sailing vessel and sank in twelve minutes. All four girls drowned. Anna survived and cabled him two words: "Saved alone."

Spafford sailed immediately to meet his wife. When the ship passed over the place where his daughters had drowned, he wrote the words of a hymn. Philip Bliss — who is also on this site — set them to music: "When peace like a river attendeth my way, / When sorrows like sea billows roll; / Whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say, / It is well, it is well with my soul."

The hymn does not deny the grief. It claims something the grief cannot reach. Spafford later lost a son to scarlet fever. He died in Jerusalem in 1888, aged fifty-nine, having served the poor there for seven years.
family_story:
Horatio Spafford was an American lawyer and friend of the preacher D.L. Moody.

In 1873, his four daughters drowned when their ship sank crossing the Atlantic. His wife survived. She cabled him two words: "Saved alone."

Spafford sailed to meet her. As his ship crossed the same stretch of ocean, he wrote the words of a hymn that Philip Bliss — also on this site — set to music:

"When sorrows like sea billows roll;
Whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say,
It is well, it is well with my soul."

The hymn does not pretend the grief is gone. It says: even now, God is good.

name: John Wesley
footnote: Wesley taught the doctrine of 'entire sanctification' or 'Christian perfection' — the belief that a second work of grace can enable believers to love God and neighbour as their governing motive. This is distinct from sinless perfection and does not contradict salvation by grace through faith, but it is a significant Arminian distinctive that Reformed and Calvinist evangelicals would question. His Aldersgate conversion and the thrust of his preaching were firmly evangelical.
source_summary: John Wesley (1703–1791) was an English Anglican clergyman, theologian, and evangelist who founded the Methodist movement. Born the fifteenth child of Samuel and Susanna Wesley in Epworth, Lincolnshire, he studied at Oxford, was ordained an Anglican priest, and sailed to Georgia in 1735 as a missionary. He experienced evangelical conversion at a Moravian meeting in Aldersgate Street on 24 May 1738 — writing 'I felt my heart strangely warmed' and 'I felt I did trust in Christ, Christ alone for salvation.' He subsequently preached more than 40,000 sermons, travelled approximately 250,000 miles on horseback across Britain and Ireland, and founded Methodist societies that became a global movement. He wrote his final letter to William Wilberforce — urging him not to abandon the fight against slavery — a week before his death at age 87.
adult_story:
John Wesley was born in Epworth, Lincolnshire in 1703, the fifteenth child of Samuel and Susanna Wesley. He studied at Oxford, was ordained an Anglican priest, and sailed to Georgia in 1735 as a missionary — a venture that ended in failure and his return to England deeply uncertain about his own standing before God.

The turning point came on 24 May 1738. At a Moravian meeting in Aldersgate Street, London, Wesley heard Luther's preface to Romans read aloud. He wrote afterwards: “I felt my heart strangely warmed. I felt I did trust in Christ, Christ alone for salvation.” From that evening he preached the grace he had previously only theorised. With the open fields as his pulpit — following George Whitefield’s example — he preached more than 40,000 sermons over fifty years, travelling 250,000 miles on horseback throughout Britain and Ireland. His Methodist societies became the seed of a global movement.

A week before his death in 1791 he wrote his final letter to William Wilberforce, urging him not to abandon the fight against slavery. He died at eighty-seven, still trusting the Christ whose grace had warmed his heart fifty-three years before. For all his formidable organisation and energy, the thing that drove him was a single conviction: that a grace sufficient for John Wesley was sufficient for anyone.
family_story:
John Wesley was born in England in 1703, one of nineteen children. He became a minister and even sailed to America as a missionary — but he came home unsure he truly trusted God.

One evening in London in 1738, he heard someone read from the Bible about trusting Jesus alone. Something changed inside him. He wrote that his heart felt “strangely warmed” — and he knew Jesus had saved him.

After that, John spent fifty years on horseback all across Britain, preaching wherever people would listen. He gave more than 40,000 sermons. He never stopped pointing people to the grace that had changed him.

name: George Verwer
source_summary: George Verwer (1938–2023) was an American evangelical missionary and founder of Operation Mobilisation (OM). Born in New Jersey, he converted to faith in Christ at sixteen at a Billy Graham meeting in Madison Square Garden in 1955. He began distributing Gospel literature in Mexico in 1957 and formally founded OM in 1962. Under his leadership OM grew to thousands of workers in over a hundred countries. He launched OM's ship ministry — MV Logos (1970) and MV Doulos (1977) — using vessels as floating bookshops to distribute Christian literature at ports worldwide. He coined the term 'messiology' to describe his conviction that God works through imperfect people. He handed leadership to Peter Maiden in 2003 and died in London on 14 April 2023, aged 84.
adult_story:
George Verwer was born in New Jersey in 1938 and converted to faith in Christ at sixteen at a Billy Graham meeting in Madison Square Garden. The following year he began distributing Gospel literature in Mexico with a handful of friends; by 1962 he had formally founded Operation Mobilisation, which would grow to thousands of workers in more than a hundred countries.

Verwer’s two great convictions shaped everything OM became. The first was that the gospel was owed to the whole world, not a comfortable few. The second was what he called “messiology” — his term for the biblical truth that God works through fallible, stumbling people, not around them. He launched ship ministries to take the gospel to ports inaccessible to conventional mission teams: the MV Logos in 1970, the Doulos in 1977. He called himself “God’s Bungler” — not in false modesty but as theology.

He handed OM’s leadership over in 2003 but continued preaching, writing, and encouraging missionaries until shortly before his death in London on 14 April 2023, aged eighty-four. His legacy is hundreds of thousands of lives reached and a generation of workers from every continent who took seriously the same passage that had once gripped a sixteen-year-old in Madison Square Garden: Christ died for sins, once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God.
family_story:
George Verwer was born in New Jersey in 1938. At sixteen, he went to a Billy Graham meeting and gave his life to Jesus. After that, one thing burned in him: the whole world needed to hear the gospel.

He started handing out Bibles in Mexico with a few friends. That small team grew into Operation Mobilisation — thousands of workers in over a hundred countries. He even bought ships to take the gospel to ports around the world.

George called himself “God’s Bungler” — because God uses imperfect people. He kept serving until he died in London in 2023, aged eighty-four.
